---
title: Calibrating selection of comparative biogeographic models with hyperpriors
authors:
  - name: João Fentanes Machado
    affiliation:
      - id: fsp
        name: Universidade Federal de São Paulo
        city: São Paulo
        country: Brazil
  - name: Jamie Oaks
    orcid: 0000-0002-3757-3836
    url: https://phyletica.org
    corresponding: true
    email: joaks@auburn.edu
    affiliation:
      - id: aumnh
        name: Auburn University Museum of Natural History
        city: Auburn
        state: AL
        country: USA
        url: https://aumnh.auburn.edu/
      - id: audbs
        name: Auburn University Department of Biological Sciences
        city: Auburn
        state: AL
        country: USA
        url: https://www.auburn.edu/cosam/departments/biology/index.htm
abstract: |
  [...]In comparative biogeographic methods like msbayes and ecoevolity, prior distributions on divergence times with lots of uncertainty can create a strong
  penalty against models with more divergence-time parameters, and thus cause a downward bias in the number of divergence events across the taxa being
  compared. For this project, we will explore whether a hierarchical Bayesian approach can mitigate such a bias. Specifically, we will explore using
  hyperpriors on the parameters of the prior on divergence times to see if the model can learn about the divergence-time prior during the analysis and
  better calibrate the “penalty” against adding divergence-time parameters to the model.
  
  
  
keywords:
  - biogeography
  - comparative phylogeography
  - Bayesian model choice
  - Bayesian hyperprior
number-sections: true
---

## Introduction
Reconstructing the timing of evolutionary events is fundamental for understanding key processes in comparative phylogeography,
including the mechanisms driving lineage diversification, the emergence of biogeographic patterns, and the assembly of biodiversity
across regional landscapes. A central question in comparative phylogeography is whether codistributed lineages have responded synchronously
to shared historical events or whether each lineage has a unique divergence history [@Avise2000; @Hickerson2006MolEcol]. Distinguishing between these
scenarios requires accurate estimates of divergence times across multiple co-distributed population pairs, as well as robust statistical methods
for inferring whether those divergence times are shared or independent. 

Bayesian approaches have become the standard for estimating divergence times because they provide a principled framework for integrating prior
biological knowledge while explicitly propagating uncertainty across all model parameters [@Drummond2007; @Yang2006].
In the context of comparative phylogeography, Bayesian methods have been developed to simultaneously estimate divergence times across multiple
taxon pairs and infer the degree to which those divergences are clustered in time,  that is, whether lineages diverged synchronously or 
asynchronously [@Hickerson2006; @Oaks2018ecoevolity]. Both simulation-based [@Hickerson2006; @Huang2011] and full-likelihood [@Oaks2018ecoevolity;
@Oaks2019codemog] implementations of this framework have been proposed, enabling increasingly rigorous tests of synchronous divergence hypotheses.

However, these methods can be biased toward over-clustering divergence events (i.e., underestimating the number of divergence times)
[@Oaks2012; @Oaks2014reply]. This bias leads to spurious inferences of synchronous divergence, with potentially misleading implications for our
understanding of the historical processes structuring biodiversity. @Oaks2014reply proposed this tendency to prefer models with too few
divergence-time parameters was caused by the prior probability density placed on the divergence times. In Bayesian model choice, the likelihood
of a model is averaged over the priors, making the prior a "penalty" for adding parameters to the model. When the prior on divergence times
is broad, as is necessary when there is genuine prior uncertainty about when lineages diverged, it places low prior probability density on any
specific divergence time, imposing a strong implicit penalty against models that infer many distinct events. As a result, models that collapse
multiple divergence times into fewer shared events are systematically favored, not because the data support synchrony, but because the broad 
prior penalizes complexity.

Likelihood and ABC methods for estimating synchronous divergences are sensitive to the prior probability density placed on the divergence times
of the population pairs[@Siler2012;@Oaks2014reply;@Oaks2014dpp;@Oaks2019codemog]. A prior with a large variance to express prior uncertainty
about divergence times can act as a strong penalty against models with more divergence times among the pairs of populations, causing the
analysis to over-cluster taxa to shared divergences. Yet specifying a narrow prior requires strong a priori knowledge about divergence times
that is rarely available in practice, creating a difficult trade-off between expressing appropriate uncertainty and avoiding inferential bias.
A natural solution to this problem is to move the uncertainty up one level in the model hierarchy: rather than fixing the parameters of the
divergence-time prior, one can place hyperpriors over those parameters and allow the data themselves to inform the most appropriate prior
during inference.

Here, to overcome this limitation we implement such a hierarchical model in ecoevolity [@Oaks2018ecoevolity], placing hyperpriors on the parameters of the divergence-time
prior so that the scale and shape of that prior are estimated jointly with all other model parameters. This approach allows the model to
adaptively learn an appropriate prior on divergence times from the data, avoiding the need to commit to a fixed prior whose breadth may
systematically bias the analysis. Using simulated comparative biogeographic data and empirical genomic data from Philippine geckos, we
(i) characterize the downward bias in the estimated number of divergence events caused by prior uncertainty about the timing of divergence,
and (ii) evaluate whether hyperpriors on the divergence-time prior parameters effectively mitigate this bias and recover the true number of
divergence events.

## Methods
### Ecoevolity implementation of hyperpriors on divergence-time prior parameters

#### Prior sampling validation and coverage analysis
Prior to evaluating the performance of hyperpriors in mitigating divergence-time estimation bias, we
validated the ecoevolity implementation of hyperpriors on divergence-time prior parameters through two
complementary checks. (i) Prior check: To confirm that ecoevolity correctly samples from the specified
prior distribution in the absence of data, we ran the MCMC sampler with no likelihood contribution from
the data, effectively sampling from the prior alone. Under this condition, the resulting marginal distributions
of all parameters should match the analytically expected prior distributions. Significant deviations would indicate
an implementation error in the prior specification or the sampler itself. (ii) Coverage check: To validate that the
posterior credible intervals produced by ecoevolity achieve the correct nominal coverage when analyzing data simulated
under a model with divergence-time hyperpriors, we simulated datasets under two hyperpriors on divergence times - τ ~ 
Exponential(mean ~ Exponential(mean = 0.2)) and τ ~ Uniform(0, max ~ Uniform(0, 0.2)) - and analyzed each under the
corresponding hyperprior model. For each parameter, we recorded whether the true value, used to simulate the data,
fell within the 50% credible interval. Across all simulations, the proportion of times the true value was contained
within the credible interval should approximate 0.5 if the implementation is correct. Systematic under or overcoverage
would indicate miscalibration of the posterior. 

### Demonstrating bias imposed by vague divergence-time priors
To demonstrate this with simulated data, we simulated data under a more informative distribution on divergence times and then analyzed those data
sets using a more “vague” prior on divergence times. If our hypothesis about the cause of the downward bias on the number of divergence time is correct,
we expected the results of these analyses to show a tendency to underestimate the number of divergence events across pairs of populations. We will also
analyze each dataset under the true (more informative) prior distribution to serve as a best-case scenario for comparison.

We evaluated two families of divergence-time distributions: exponential, because waiting times for random events tend
to be exponentially distributed, and uniform, because the downward bias in estimating the number of divergence events
was first recognized in the context of uniform priors on divergence times [@Oaks2012,@Oaks2014dpp,@Hickerson2013,@Oaks2014reply]
For the exponential family, we simulated datasets each comprising 20 pairs of populations, each represented by biallelic codominant
marker data summarized across 20,000 sites, under τ ~ Exponential(mean = 0.01) substitutions per site. Each dataset was then analyzed
under three models: (i) a fixed vague prior τ ~ Exponential(mean = 0.2), which mismatches the true distribution; (ii) a hyperprior model
τ ~ Exponential(mean ~ Exponential (mean = 0.2)), which places an exponentially distributed hyperprior on the mean of the divergence-time
prior, expressing greater uncertainty while allowing the data to inform the prior scale; and (iii) the true distribution τ ~ Exponential
(mean = 0.01), serving as a best-case reference for comparison. For the uniform family, we simulated datasets each comprising 20 pairs of
populations, each represented by biallelic codominant marker data summarized across 20,000 sites, under τ ~ Uniform(0, 0.02) substitutions
per site. Each dataset was analyzed under: (i) a fixed vague prior τ ~ Uniform(0, 0.2); (ii) a hyperprior model τ ~ Uniform(0, max ~ Uniform
(0, 0.2)), which places a uniformly distributed hyperprior on the upper bound of the divergence-time prior, expressing equivalent uncertainty
to the vague prior while allowing the data to inform the upper limit; and (iii) the true distribution τ ~ Uniform(0, 0.02) as a best-case reference.
Model performance was assessed via the MAP estimate of the number of divergence events, the probability of underestimation p(k̂ < k), coverage of the
95% credible set p(k ∈ CS₀.₉₅), median posterior probability of the true event count, and mean model distance.

### Empirical application using Philippine gekkonid datasets
We evaluated the performance of the hyperprior on the divergence-time prior using restriction site-associated DNA sequencing (RADseq) datasets from Philippine
gekkonids of the genera Cyrtodactylus and Gekko, originally generated by [@Oaks2018paic]. These datasets comprise populations sampled across multiple islands in
the Philippine archipelago and have previously been used to investigate the role of island fragmentation in shaping diversification patterns. In the original study,
a full-likelihood Bayesian model-choice framework was employed to test for temporally clustered divergence events associated with the complex geological history of the
archipelago [@Oaks2018paic].

To assess the sensitivity of the empirical analyses to the prior specification, we reanalyzed these datasets under a range of prior distributions for the Dirichlet process
concentration parameter. This allowed us to evaluate the influence of the concentration prior on posterior estimates of divergence models and to determine whether the empirical
data were sufficiently informative to overcome prior assumptions.

## Results

### Validating MCMC sampling from prior distribution

To validate the ecoevolity implementation of divergence-time hyperpriors, if we look at the the MCMC samples 
(fig-prior-hyper-exp-nevents) we first confirmed that the MCMC sampler correctly recovers the specified prior
distributions in the absence of data @fig-priorcheck-mcmc. For the exponential hyperprior
model, the empirical distribution of the time-prior mean showed excellent agreement
with the expected Exponential(mean = 0.2) distribution (KS D = 0.0099, p = 0.28),
and the marginal distribution of the number of divergence events was likewise
consistent with the model expectation (KS D = 0.0076, p = 0.67). Similarly,
for the uniform hyperprior model, the empirical distribution of the time-prior
maximum closely matched the expected Uniform(0, 0.2) distribution (KS D = 0.012, p = 0.12),
and the number of divergence events showed no significant deviation from the prior expectation
(KS D = 0.0097, p = 0.35). In all cases, the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test failed to detect significant
departures between empirical and expected distributions, confirming that ecoevolity correctly
samples from the specified hyperprior distributions when likelihood contributions from the data are removed.

![
    The cumulative density of the MCMC samples and the expected prior distribution of the model match
    This is shown for the mean (A) and max (B) parameters for the exponential and uniform priors, respectively, on
    divergence times and for the number of divergence events under the exponential (C) and uniform (D)
    hyperpriors on divergence times. Each plot includes the results of a Kolmogorov-Smirnov test, which was
    unable to reject that ecoevolity sampled from the expected distribution.
](
    ./images/fig1-priorcheck-mcmc.svg
){
    #fig-priorcheck-mcmc
    fig-alt="MCMCsampling"
    fig-align="center"
}

### Coverage of credible intervals
Coverage checks confirmed that ecoevolity accurately recovers hyperprior parameters and divergence times @fig-coverage-figure-2-pdf. Under the 
exponential hyperprior, the time-prior mean showed near-nominal 50% credible interval coverage (p(μ ∈ CI₀.₅₀) = 0.55;
RMSE = 0.058; Figure 9A), and divergence times were well-calibrated (p(τ ∈ CI₀.₅₀) = 0.49; RMSE = 0.065; Figure 9B).
Under the uniform hyperprior, the time-prior upper bound was recovered with coverage close to nominal (p(x ∈ CI₀.₅₀)
= 0.46; RMSE = 0.0087; Figure 9C), and divergence times showed near-perfect calibration (p(τ ∈ CI₀.₅₀) = 0.50; RMSE
= 0.0011; Figure 9D), with no significant bias (p(τ̂ < τ) = 0.49; p = 0.51). These results confirm that ecoevolity
produces accurate and well-calibrated estimates under both hyperprior specification 

![
    Coverage check for the ecoevolity implementation of divergence-time hyperpriors. Each point represents one simulation
    replicate, with the true parameter value on the x-axis and the posterior mean estimate on the y-axis; error bars denote
    50% credible intervals. Panels show the time-prior mean (A) and mean divergence time (B) under the exponential hyperprior
    τ ~ Exponential(mean ~ Exponential(mean = 0.2)), and the time-prior upper bound (C) and mean divergence time (D) under the
    uniform hyperprior τ ~ Uniform(0, max ~ Uniform(0, 0.2)). The diagonal line represents perfect recovery. Statistics include
    the probability of underestimation, proportion of true values within the 50% credible interval, and root mean square error (RMSE).
](
    ./images/coverage-pdf-bc-svg-corrupted.pdf
){
    #fig-coverage-figure-2-pdf
    fig-alt="SVG-coverage-figure-is-working?"
    fig-align="center"
}

### Hyper prior estimates on divergence time using simulated data
The introduction of hyperpriors on the divergence-time prior parameters substantially mitigated the underestimation bias observed under
fixed vague priors (Figure 11). For the exponential prior, placing a hyperprior on the mean of the exponential prior, τ ~ Exponential (μ),
where μ ~ Exponential(mean = 0.2), reduced the probability of underestimation from 0.97 to 0.42, and restored 95% credible set coverage to
0.99 (Figure 11A, middle panel). Performance under the hyperprior was nearly identical to that of the true prior used in simulations, τ ~
Exponential(mean = 0.01), which achieved p(k̂ < k) = 0.40 and coverage of 0.98 (Figure 11A, right panel). A parallel pattern was observed for
the uniform prior: placing a hyperprior on the upper bound of the uniform prior, τ ~ Uniform(0, 2), where Uniform (0, Uniform(0, 0.2)), reduced
underestimation from 0.97 to 0.39 and improved coverage from 0.19 to 0.95 (Figure 11B, middle panel), again approaching the performance of the
true prior (coverage = 0.98; Figure 11B, right panel).

![
    Performance of ecoevolity under fixed and hyperprior divergence-time models. Heatmaps show the joint distribution
    of the true versus MAP-estimated number of divergence events across simulation replicates for the exponential (A)
    and uniform (B) prior families. Color intensity reflects the frequency of each true-MAP combination. For each prior
    family, three models are compared: a fixed vague prior (left; Exp(mean = 0.2) and Unif(0, 0.2)), a hyperprior model 
    (middle; Exp(mean ~ Exp(mean = 0.2)) and Unif(0, Unif(0, 0.2))), and the true reference prior used to simulate the data
    (right; Exp(mean = 0.01) and Unif(0, 0.02)). Statistics reported in each panel include the probability of underestimation
    p(k̂ < k), the proportion of true values falling within the 95% credible set p(k ∈ CS₀.₉₅), and the median posterior probability
    of the true number of divergence events median p(k|D)
](
    ./images/hyperprior-ecoevolityperformance-simulated-data.svg
){
    #fig-ecoevolityperformance-simulated
    fig-alt="simulated data performance"
    fig-align="center"
}

### Empirical application to Philippine Gekkonids
We used restriction site-associated DNA sequencing (RADseq) data from species *Cyrtodactylus* and *Gekko*.
Results: Big difference when using the hyperprior.
(@pycoevolity-only-nevents-time-empirical-dataset)

![
    Empirical dataset
](
    ./images/pycoevolity-only-nevents-time-empirical-dataset.svg
){
    #fig-gecko-nevents-time
    fig-alt="gecko-nevents-time"
    fig-align="center"
}

## Discussion

## References

::: {#refs}
:::

# Supporting Information
